hook.text: In the U.S., employer health insurance started during WWII—when wage freezes forced companies to compete with benefits instead.

buildup.text: Health insurance is the most valued employee benefit—covering medical, dental, and vision care for workers and families.

discovery.text: Employers typically cover 70-80% of premiums. The average employer cost is over $16,000 per employee per year.

twist.text: Rising healthcare costs are the biggest benefits challenge—outpacing inflation and squeezing budgets for other investments.

climax.text: Companies manage costs through plan design—deductibles, copays, HSAs, and wellness programs—while maintaining coverage quality.

punchline.text: Health insurance: a WWII accident that became the most important benefit.

quiz.question: Why did employer-sponsored health insurance start in the U.S.?
options: WWII wage freezes forced companies to compete with benefits
